title: Sales
text:
As an Appliance Category Advisor, Arturo supports customers through high-consideration purchases by explaining product value, comparing options, and recommending solutions that fit each customer’s needs. His sales approach is consultative: the goal is not just to sell a product, but to help the customer understand why a product is the right fit.
This experience connects directly to his business and marketing education. Arturo understands that strong sales work depends on customer insight, product positioning, communication, and trust.

title: M08-Reflection Essay-Advanced Data Types
text:
Cumsum(is.na()) & first()

this combination allowed him to turn invisible formatting gaps into hard, indexable data groups

str_remove()

This allows cleaning trailing dots and artifacts using regex dollar signs.

fct_reorder(.fun = last)

This helps align line charts legends to the right edge of the data








His presentation covered the anatomy of an automated graphic. He covers a handful of different key points to help wrangle data and utilize new functions to present said data more clearly for our viewers and audience. To be honest the presentation was fairly easy to digest and understand just very jam packed with a lot to take in all in at once. The two of them definitely have their own style cues but I enjoyed both. I did like both but I would say I preferred David Robinson’s presentation more., I liked it because I liked the EDA Survival ToolKit and liked the fact that a lot of the functions he discussed where something I feel I would use in my career environment a little more but only time will tell.






Well for starters, the key capabilities would be automatic summary tables, group comparisons, Regression model summaries and Publication-ready formatting. All of these key capabilities help make the gtsummary package what it is whcih allows it to quickly create clean, professional summary tables for data analysis. I feel it would benefit me in both school and work. For school it would make my assignments look a little more professional, could even cut down on manual calculations for statistics, and make it easier to interpret results clearly. For work, it could speed up my workflow, allows me to produce publication-quality tables to share among my colleagues or managers.






The {gtsummary} package automates statistical summaries and model outputs, while {gt} and {gtExtras} maily focus on formatting and visual styling.

Automatic detection and handling of variable types (including special behavior for dichotomous variables).
Direct support for regression models with correct statistical outputs and formatting.
Strong integation with reproducible workflows (R Markdown, exporting and inline reporting).








Strings: str_sub() it lets you take a portion of the text from a string based on position.

Factor: fct_reorder() is rearranges the order of categories (factors) so they make more sense_ especially for plots


Date and Time: make_date() and make_datetime() are used to create dates and date-times from separate components.








At first, for me wrangling data seemed very tedious but after learning some cool things throughout the module it made it somewhat enjoyable and rewarding when I completed an assignment. I personally enjoyed learning all the new packages from the lectures, yes they were very long and felt time consuming but still enjoyed learning some new things that can help make assignments feel stream-lined. Like previously stating that I enjoyed fct_reorder() from the forcats package becasue it stood out to me because it directly improved final result looks and reinterpreted the data. Also enjoyed using str_sub() or make_datetime() becasue they are vert intuitive and easier to remember. Overall not too much to complain about from this module except for the long lecture videos but in the end they made data wrangling easier for me and easier to analysis for my future audience.






I put in a pretty good amount for time into my assignments. But, I would say there have been numerous times that I would like more clarification on assignments. It feels at times that I have to really have to teach myself step by step and it takes up a lot of my time especially that I work full-time and take care of both my parents.

title: Module 3-1- Principle - Data visualization with ggplot in R
text:
After taking this workshop, participants should be able to do following:


Explain the concept of the grammar of graphics when visualizing data with the ggplot2 package.
Be familiar with various types of charts.
Visualize data in counts and proportions.
Select appropriate charts based on strategic considerations (e.g., the characteristics of the data and audience).
Create a chart that involves one or two variables with either categorical or continuous data.
Create a chart by adding a categorical moderator (3rd variable) to the chart involving two or three variables.
Create correlation charts.
Read charts and generate insights.
Describe three popular packages that allow one to visualize data.
Explain the concept of the grammar of graphics when visualizing data with the ggplot2 package.

A data frame with 32 observations on 11 (numeric) variables.

Miles/(US) gallon

Number of cylinders

Number of forward gears

Number of carburetors

[1] "data.frame"

#short cut for pipe operator (%>%, |>) : ctl + shift + M

cars <- mtcars
  mtcars %>% # piping operator from dplyr (shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+M)
    rownames_to_column() %>% # do this before changing the data to tibble as the conversion will remove rownames in tibble.
    as_tibble() %>%  
    rename(model = rowname) %>% 
    print (n = 20, width = Inf)

title: Module 3-1- Principle - Data visualization with ggplot in R
Module 3-1- Principle - Data visualization with ggplot in R
text:
4.1 Elaborate Examples

4.1.1 x & y are both continuous with moderator & labeller()

wrangling

cars |> 
  count(cyl)

  cyl  n
1   4 11
2   6  7
3   8 14




plotting

cars |>
  mutate(cyl = factor(cyl)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = mpg, y = disp, colour = cyl)) + 
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ cyl, ncol = 1) +
  theme_bw()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Module 3-1- Principle - Data visualization with ggplot in R

Quarto
Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see https://quarto.org.

title: Module 3-1- Principle - Data visualization with ggplot in R
text:
Running Code
When you click the Render button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

1 + 1

[1] 2


You can add options to executable code like this


[1] 4


The echo: false option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

Tip



Hint: Note that this CSV file is messy. Open it up in Excel and examine it. Each row has only one column that contains five elements separated by “;”. There is a way to parse them nicely during the importation process by adjusting the setting of read_csv() function. Inside the function, you will want to add appropriate arguments. For details, look up help by typing “?read_csv”. Alternatively, you may use the menu to generate codes to execute (import > dataset > from Text(readr)). After you read the file into R, you may change data types. For instance, “order_date” variable can be nicely converted to date data type using the lubridate package introduced in M07 and M08.

Create a subdirectory called “images” under the project folder such that it is on the same level as “data” subdirectory. Next, save it as several different graphics files, such as png, pdf, and jpeg. Make sure the files are saved into the “images” subdirectory.
text:
Q2.2: Using the data imported earlier, create a chart showing the count of the quantity of the items ordered. Create a subdirectory called “images” under the project folder such that it is on the same level as “data” subdirectory. Next, save it as several different graphics files, such as png, pdf, and jpeg. Make sure the files are saved into the “images” subdirectory.

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Create images folder if it does not already exist
if (!dir.exists("images")) {
  dir.create("images")
}

# Create chart
item_plot <- item_excel %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = item, y = quantity)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Quantity of Items Ordered",
    x = "Item",
    y = "Quantity"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Next, by following the procedure explained in the video, create a vector called “top_movies,” which is the top 250 movies shown on the web page. When you do this, try to read and create the “top_movies” dataset in one chain of piping operators. Next, clean up the vector by deleting blank rows.

The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

library(stringr)

url <- "https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/"

top_movies <- url %>%
  read_html() %>%
  html_elements(".titleColumn a") %>%
  html_text(trim = TRUE) %>%
  .[. != ""]

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q4.2: Now, scrape the year and assign it “year” as a variable name by applying the same procedure used to extract the “top_movies” dataset above. Next, clean the data.

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q4.3: Still on this same URL, now scrape the rating and name it “rating” as a variable name. If there is any other extra information, clean the data.

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Create a data frame called “top250_movies” by combining the three vectors you created earlier – “top_movies,” “year,” and “rating.” Then save the data after sorting it in descending order of rating. Confirm that your work was done correctly by printing the top and bottom 6 rows.
text:
Create a data frame called “top250_movies” by combining the three vectors you created earlier – “top_movies,” “year,” and “rating.” Then save the data after sorting it in descending order of rating. Confirm that your work was done correctly by printing the top and bottom 6 rows.

library(dplyr)

# Combine into data frame
top250_movies <- data.frame(
  top_movies = top_movies,
  year = year,
  rating = rating
)

# Convert types (just in case)
top250_movies <- top250_movies %>%
  mutate(
    year = as.numeric(year),
    rating = as.numeric(rating)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(rating))   # sort by highest rating

# View top and bottom 6 rows
head(top250_movies, 6)

                top_movies year rating
1 The Shawshank Redemption 1994    9.2
2            The Godfather 1972    9.1
3   The Godfather: Part II 1974    9.0
4          The Dark Knight 2008    9.0
5             12 Angry Men 1957    8.9
6         Schindler's List 1993    8.9

tail(top250_movies, 6)

               top_movies year rating
245              Drishyam 2013      8
246 The Battle of Algiers 1966      8
247     Nights of Cabiria 1957      8
248 Miracle in Cell No. 7 2019      8
249         Andrei Rublev 1966      8
250    The Princess Bride 1987      8








Tip



Hint: To combine the three vectors, the number of elements in each vector should be the same. Check it first. One way to do it is by passing the vectors in length() function – e.g., length(top_movies). Of course, you can visually inspect the vectors by printing them on the screen using the print() function or simply typing the vector name and executing it. If you converted the vector to a data frame, length() won’t work; use nrow() instead.

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q6.2: Replace all elements with “N/A”, “N A”, or “N / A” with a missing value in R (e.g., “NA”) across all character variables. Use the four different functions to do the task.
text:
Q6.2: Replace all elements with “N/A”, “N A”, or “N / A” with a missing value in R (e.g., “NA”) across all character variables. Use the four different functions to do the task.

library(dplyr)

dis_exec_clean <- dis_exec_clean %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~case_when(
    .x %in% c("N/A", "N A", "N / A") ~ NA_character_,
    TRUE ~ .x
  )))

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q6.4: Out of the four functions you tried, Which one is your favorite function and why?
text:
Q6.4: Out of the four functions you tried, Which one is your favorite function and why?
My favorite function is na_if() because it’s the easiest to use and very straightforward. It does exactly what I need without extra complexity. The other functions work too, but they require more code and are harder to read. na_if() is cleaner and more efficient for this type of task.

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q7.1: For student/teacher ratio, use the following URL to read the data: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2019/2019-05-07/student_teacher_ratio.csv Links to an external site.. Examine the data. Explore the data to answer the following: (1) variables with missing values, (2) the years covered in the data and their distribution, (3) all the indicators and their distribution. Next, filter the data to include rows with 2016 “Secondary Education” and without missing values for “student_ratio.” Save the data as “s_t_ratio_2nd_edu_2016”. This new dataset should have 151 rows and 8 columns.
text:
Q7.1: For student/teacher ratio, use the following URL to read the data: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2019/2019-05-07/student_teacher_ratio.csv Links to an external site.. Examine the data. Explore the data to answer the following: (1) variables with missing values, (2) the years covered in the data and their distribution, (3) all the indicators and their distribution. Next, filter the data to include rows with 2016 “Secondary Education” and without missing values for “student_ratio.” Save the data as “s_t_ratio_2nd_edu_2016”. This new dataset should have 151 rows and 8 columns.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)

# Step 1: Import data
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/master/data/2019/2019-05-07/student_teacher_ratio.csv"

s_t_ratio <- read_csv(url)

chr (6): edulit_ind, indicator, country_code, country, flag_codes, flags
dbl (2): year, student_ratio

ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

# Examine structure
glimpse(s_t_ratio)

Columns: 8

$ flags         <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …

s_t_ratio_2nd_edu_2016 <- s_t_ratio %>%
  filter(

    indicator == "Secondary Education",

  )

  filter(region != "Aggregates")

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q7.4: Draw a chart that shows the impact of GDP on the student/teacher ratio and do all the things to make the chart presentable. This includes, but is not limited to, mapping population to the size of data points and region to the color of the data points, adding country names next to the data points as a label, transformation of data, addition of linear regression line as well as all the usual labels for x, y, title, subtitle, etc.
text:
Q7.4: Draw a chart that shows the impact of GDP on the student/teacher ratio and do all the things to make the chart presentable. This includes, but is not limited to, mapping population to the size of data points and region to the color of the data points, adding country names next to the data points as a label, transformation of data, addition of linear regression line as well as all the usual labels for x, y, title, subtitle, etc.

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(scales)


Attaching package: 'scales'


The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    discard


The following object is masked from 'package:readr':

    col_factor

# Join datasets
combined_data <- s_t_ratio_2nd_edu_2016 %>%
  left_join(indicators_2016, by = c("country_code" = "iso3c"))

# Check column names
names(combined_data)

 [1] "edulit_ind"     "indicator"      "country_code"   "country.x"     
 [5] "year"           "student_ratio"  "flag_codes"     "flags"         
 [9] "country.y"      "region"         "gdp_per_capita" "population"    

# Create a clean country label column
# Change country.y below to country.x if needed
plot_data <- combined_data %>%
  mutate(country_label = country.y) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(gdp_per_capita),
    !is.na(population),
    !is.na(student_ratio),
    !is.na(country_label),
    gdp_per_capita > 0
  )

# Plot
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = student_ratio)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = population, color = region), alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = country_label, color = region), size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 10), labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Impact of GDP per Capita on Student-Teacher Ratio in 2016",
    subtitle = "Secondary education student-teacher ratio across countries",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale, current US$)",
    y = "Student-Teacher Ratio",
    color = "Region",
    size = "Population",
    caption = "Source: World Bank WDI and student-teacher ratio data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: M09-2-Application Assignment: Data Import and Export
section: Q7.5: This time, create a separate chart by region. There should be six charts. Also, do all the appropriate coding to improve the aesthetics of the chart.
text:
Q7.5: This time, create a separate chart by region. There should be six charts. Also, do all the appropriate coding to improve the aesthetics of the chart.

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(scales)

# Join datasets
combined_data <- s_t_ratio_2nd_edu_2016 %>%
  left_join(indicators_2016, by = c("country_code" = "iso3c"))

# Create clean plotting data
# If country.y does not exist, replace it with the correct country-name column
plot_data <- combined_data %>%
  mutate(country_label = country.y) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(gdp_per_capita),
    !is.na(population),
    !is.na(student_ratio),
    !is.na(region),
    !is.na(country_label),
    gdp_per_capita > 0
  )

# Faceted chart by region
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = student_ratio)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = population, color = region), alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country_label),
    size = 2.5,
    max.overlaps = 15,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 8), labels = comma) +
  facet_wrap(~ region) +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita and Student-Teacher Ratio by Region (2016)",
    subtitle = "Secondary education student-teacher ratio across countries",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale, current US$)",
    y = "Student-Teacher Ratio",
    size = "Population",
    color = "Region",
    caption = "Source: World Bank WDI and student-teacher ratio data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: About
text:
Arturo Rosas is a customer service, sales, and project coordination professional based in Southern California. His experience centers on helping customers make informed decisions, coordinating expectations across clients and contractors, and supporting teams through training and product knowledge.
He currently works as an Appliance Category Advisor at Best Buy in West Covina, CA, where he delivers tailored customer solutions, resolves complex concerns, and mentors team members.
Arturo is pursuing a Bachelor of Science in International Business & Marketing at California State Polytechnic University, Pomona, with expected graduation in 2027. He previously earned associate degrees in Business Administration and Social and Behavioral Sciences from Mt. San Antonio College in 2023.
